title: A Bacteriophage Capsid Protein Is an Inhibitor of a Conserved Transcription Terminator of Various Bacterial Pathogens
abstract: Rho is a hexameric molecular motor that functions as a conserved transcription terminator in the majority of bacterial species and is a potential drug target. Psu is a bacteriophage P4 capsid protein that inhibits Escherichia coli Rho by obstructing its ATPase and translocase activities. In this study, we explored the anti-Rho activity of Psu for Rho proteins from different pathogens. Sequence alignment and homology modeling of Rho proteins from pathogenic bacteria revealed the conserved nature of the Psu-interacting regions in all these proteins. We chose Rho proteins from various pathogens, including Mycobacterium smegmatis , Mycobacterium bovis , Mycobacterium tuberculosis , Xanthomonas campestris , Xanthomonas oryzae , Corynebacterium glutamicum , Vibrio cholerae , Salmonella enterica , and Pseudomonas syringae . The purified recombinant Rho proteins of these organisms showed variable rates of ATP hydrolysis on poly(rC) as the substrate and were capable of releasing RNA from the E. coli transcription elongation complexes. Psu was capable of inhibiting these two functions of all these Rho proteins. In vivo pulldown assays revealed direct binding of Psu with many of these Rho proteins. In vivo expression of psu induced killing of M. smegmatis , M. bovis , X. campestris , and E. coli expressing S. enterica Rho indicating Psu-induced inhibition of Rho proteins of these strains under physiological conditions. We propose that the “universal” inhibitory function of the Psu protein against the Rho proteins from both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria could be useful for designing peptides with antimicrobial functions and that these peptides could contribute to synergistic antibiotic treatment of the pathogens by compromising the Rho functions. IMPORTANCE Bacteriophage-derived protein factors modulating different bacterial processes could be converted into unique antimicrobial agents. Bacteriophage P4 capsid protein Psu is an inhibitor of the E. coli transcription terminator Rho. Here we show that apart from antagonizing E. coli Rho, Psu is able to inhibit Rho proteins from various phylogenetically unrelated Gram-negative and Gram-positive pathogens. Upon binding to these Rho proteins, Psu inhibited them by affecting their ATPase and RNA release functions. The expression of Psu in vivo kills various pathogens, such as Mycobacterium and Xanthomonas species. Hence, Psu could be useful for identifying peptide sequences with anti-Rho activities and might constitute part of synergistic antibiotic treatment against pathogens.

INTRODUCTION.content: Rho-dependent transcription termination in bacteria is an important step in the regulation of gene expression (1–5). The homohexameric transcription terminator Rho is an RNA-dependent helicase motor. Upon its association with therutsite on nascent RNA emerging from the elongation complex (EC) (6,7), Rho becomes ATPase competent, translocates along the RNA toward the EC, and eventually dislodges the latter (8). Recent studies have revealed that Rho-dependent termination is involved and instrumental in regulating many physiological processes (9). This multifaceted functional outcome of the Rho-dependent termination process makes Rho an ideal target of different bactericidal agents. With the emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) and extensively drug resistant (XDR) strains, it has become necessary to look for alternative avenues and additional drug targets. It is likely that due to its involvement in many physiological pathways, Rho inhibition could be part of a synergistic antibacterial treatment strategy.

Psu (polaritysuppression) is an unconventional capsid organizing protein of bacteriophage P4 that moonlights as a specific inhibitor of Rho (10). It binds and antagonizes Rho intransby creating a mechanical hindrance to Rho translocation (11) via a physical blockade of the RNA exit point of the Rho central channel upon the formation of a V-shaped cap-like knotted homodimer structure (12,13). Psu is an α-helical protein with a solvent-exposed flexible C-terminal domain (CTD) (helices 6 and 7) (12) that is responsible for its association with Rho and an N-terminal domain (NTD) that sustains the stability of the protein (14). Overexpression of Psu inEscherichia coliinduces lethality due to robust antitermination at the Rho-dependent terminators throughout the genome. Rho contains an unstructured loop-forming stretch of residues in its C-terminal domain that binds with Psu (seeFig. 1B), and this binding is assisted by another helical region that surrounds the Rho central channel (13).

We hypothesized that the bacteriophage-derived Psu might function usefully as a platform for the design of new antagonists of the Rho protein, especially those made from C-terminal helices 6 and 7. So far, the antagonism of Psu has been demonstrated for the Rho protein from the model organismE. coli. In order to establish Psu as a platform for drug design, it is imperative to prove universal antagonism of Psu against various Rho proteins from both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. In this report, we have chosen and characterized the biochemical activities of the Rho proteins of selected bacterial pathogens, such asMycobacterium tuberculosis,Salmonella enterica,Vibrio cholerae,Xanthomonas oryzae,Xanthomonas campestris,Pseudomonas syringae, andCorynebacterium glutamicum.In vitrobiochemical assays showed that Psu is capable of inhibiting the ATPase functions of these Rho proteins. These Rho proteins were unable to terminate at a Rho-dependent terminator in the presence of Psu. Psu also inhibited RNA release by these Rho proteins from a stalled EC and showed direct interaction with these proteins.In vivoexpression ofpsuinMycobacterium smegmatis,Mycobacterium bovis, andX. campestrisinduced severe growth defects in the hosts and inhibited the functioning ofS. entericaRho inE. coli. These results strongly indicated that Psu is capable of functioning as a bona fide antagonist of Rho proteins from a wide spectrum of bacterial species. We further speculate that derivatives of Psu capable of functioning against the Rho functions could contribute to synergistic antibiotic treatment against bacterial pathogens.

RESULTS.content: To establish the universal antagonism of the bacteriophage P4 coat protein Psu against Rho proteins from a diverse set of bacteria, we have chosen the Gram-negative bacterial genera Salmonella , Vibrio , Xanthomonas , and Pseudomonas and the Gram-positive genera Mycobacterium and Corynebacterium . Species of these bacteria are pathogens for a wide variety of organisms: Xanthomonas oryzae (for rice), Xanthomonas campestris (for cabbage and mustard), and Pseudomonas syringae (for a wide variety of hosts) are the phytopathogens, while Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi, Vibrio cholerae , Mycobacterium tuberculosis , and Corynebacterium diphtheriae are human pathogens. For each of the human pathogens, we have carried out experiments with their nonpathogenic prototypes, such as Mycobacterium smegmatis , Mycobacterium bovis (BCG), and Corynebacterium glutamicum . The phylogenetic tree constructed on the basis of the 16S rRNA sequences of these bacterial species ( Fig. 1A ) confirms the diversity of these pathogens. The majority of these organisms are slow-growing species whose features contrast with those of E. coli , and it is expected that the Rho proteins in each of these species will function under different physiological conditions. Therefore, it is important to establish the inhibitory power of Psu against each of these Rho proteins individually. FIG 1 FIG 1 Sequential and structural homology among the Rho proteins from different pathogenic bacteria. (A) Phylogenetic tree, constructed on the basis of the 16S rRNA sequences, demonstrating the diversity of the different pathogenic bacteria from which the Rho proteins were selected for this study. (B) Sequence alignment of the region containing the Psu-binding sites from different Rho proteins, produced by Clustal Omega software. The locations of the residues responsible for binding Psu are indicated. (C) Homology models of Rho hexamers obtained by Modeler, using the crystal structure of E. coli Rho as a template (PDB 3ICE ) ( 15 ). Red loops in each structure indicate the unstructured Psu-binding region. (D) (Top) Superimposed monomer structures of all the Rho proteins, highlighting the Psu-binding loops. (Bottom) Zoomed-in view of the loops.

Earlier studies from our laboratory revealed the existence of a conserved disorganized loop region connecting the N-terminal to the C-terminal domains of E. coli Rho, which is the main site interacting with Psu ( Fig. 1B to D ) ( 13 ). A multiple-sequence alignment analysis revealed that this particular loop region is well conserved in different Rho proteins ( Fig. 1B ). To establish the structural conservation of this loop region, we built homology models of Rho proteins from different bacterial pathogens that we have chosen for our studies, using the published structure of E. coli Rho (PDB 3ICE ) as the template ( Fig. 1C and D ) ( 15 ). The modeling showed that the Psu-binding loops on different Rho proteins are surface exposed and are accessible to the solvent. However, the sizes and the finer structural details of the loops differed in the different Rho proteins. The structural conservation of the Psu-binding regions of all these Rho proteins suggests that Psu and Rho interact in these species.

The Rho proteins of the bacterial species that we chose for our study had not been characterized except for those from S. enterica ( 16 ) and M. tuberculosis ( 17 ). Therefore, it was important to characterize all these Rho proteins before attempting to establish the inhibitory property of Psu against them. We cloned all these Rho proteins into E. coli and purified the recombinant versions ( Fig. 2A ). We were unable to express M. smegmatis Rho in different BL21(DE3) cells under different conditions; thus, we proceeded to carry out in vitro experiments with M. tuberculosis Rho only. FIG 2 FIG 2 Purification and characterization of the Rho protein activities from different pathogenic bacteria. (A) SDS-PAGE analysis showing the migration patterns of recombinant Rho proteins of different species. (B to D) ATPase activities of the Rho proteins in the presence of poly(rC) as the substrate. Rho proteins with similar rates of ATPase activity are shown together in each of the panels as indicated. The activity of E. coli Rho is included in all the panels for comparison. (E) Table showing the values of the ATPase activities of all the Rho proteins. We used poly(rC), the best cofactor that induces the ATPase function of E. coli Rho, to measure the rates of the ATPase activities of all the Rho proteins ( Fig. 2B to D ). The rates are tabulated in Fig. 2E . The rate of ATPase activity of Salmonella Rho was observed to be comparable to that of E. coli Rho, whereas the rates exhibited by the Rho proteins of M. tuberculosis , C. glutamicum , and P. syringae in the presence of poly(rC) were the lowest (>10-fold lower; note the scale differences of the x axes in the different plots). The rates obtained for the Rho proteins of V. cholerae , X. campestris , and X. oryzae were 2.5- to 4-fold lower than that of their E. coli counterpart. V. cholerae , a Gram-negative bacterium, belongs to a family different from that of E. coli ( Fig. 1A ). The Rho protein of this species has several residue differences from E. coli Rho in the primary RNA binding site (PBS) (see Fig. S1 in the supplemental material). The Xanthomonas species are Gram-negative plant pathogens with high GC contents in their genomes. The X. oryzae and X. campestris Rho sequences are almost similar to each other, with only 3 amino acid differences in the PBS, while they differ considerably from their E. coli counterpart. Both the Xanthomonas species have a 7-amino-acid insertion at the beginning of the N-terminal domain (NTD) and several residue changes in the PBS, ATP-binding sites, and secondary RNA binding sites (SBS) relative to E. coli Rho. Pseudomonas syringae is also a Gram-negative plant pathogen, and its Rho protein has residue differences from E. coli Rho in both the PBS and SBS regions. The mycobacteria and corynebacteria are Gram-positive organisms with highly GC rich genomes, and their Rho proteins have an extended NTD insertion, a smaller insertion in the PBS (6 amino acids in M. tuberculosis and 11 amino acids in C. glutamicum ), and amino acid sequence differences from E. coli Rho in the PBS. Some of these organisms also have alterations in the residues in both the ATP-binding sites and the SBS (Fig. S1). Even though the homology models ( Fig. 1C and D ) suggest overall similarity in the 3-dimensional (3D) structures of all these Rho proteins, these diversities at the amino acid sequence level might have affected the sequence-specific interactions between poly(rC) and the Rho PBS. It is possible that poly(rC) is not the optimum substrate for many of these Rho proteins. Weak interactions at the PBS are likely to be the major reason for the slower ATPase activities. In addition to the sequence variations, the Rho proteins from Gram-positive bacteria have extended NTD insertions, which could have caused some effects. Moreover, all the organisms, other than Salmonella and Vibrio , have distinctly longer generation times or lower growth rates than E. coli . This specific trait is also consistent with the low ATPase activity rates of their respective Rho proteins. In spite of the wide range of ATPase activities among these Rho proteins, we have used this basic property of Rho because it is necessary for studying the effect of Psu using either poly(rC) (both long and oligomers) or natural RNA with E. coli Rho-dependent terminator sequences as the cofactor.

Psu inhibits the ATPase activity of E. coli Rho quite efficiently ( 11 ). We performed ATPase activity assays on different Rho proteins both in the presence and in the absence of Psu in a manner similar to that described above. In addition to wild-type (WT) Psu, we also used a nonfunctional Psu mutant, the ΔCTD10 mutant (with a 10-amino-acid deletion in its C-terminal domain [ 11 ]), as a negative control. Using poly(rC) as an inducer, we observed that the ATPase activities of the Rho proteins from M. tuberculosis and C. glutamicum were efficiently inhibited by Psu ( Fig. 3A ), whereas inhibition by Psu was moderate for the Rho proteins from both the Xanthomonas species. In the presence of poly(rC), we did not observe significant inhibition of the ATPase activities of the Rho proteins from the Salmonella , Pseudomonas , and Vibrio species. We repeated the experiments with a shorter poly(rC) oligonucleotide, rC 25 , to slow down ATPase activity ( 18 ). Now Psu was able to inhibit the ATPase activity of the Rho protein from the Salmonella sp. However, the ATPase functions of the Rho proteins from the Vibrio and Pseudomonas species were still only mildly affected by Psu. It should be noted that for E. coli Rho, we used a natural RNA with a λ tR1 terminator sequence, because the ATPase activity of this Rho protein on poly(rC) is too fast and could not be inhibited by Psu ( 11 ). The Psu mutant failed to inhibit any of the Rho proteins used in this study. These results indicate that in general, Psu-mediated inhibition occurred more readily when the rates of ATPase activity were low. The ineffectiveness of Psu with regard to the ATPase functions of the Pseudomonas Rho protein suggests that Psu may not be an efficient inhibitor for this protein. FIG 3 FIG 3 ATPase and in vitro transcription assays showing Psu-mediated inhibition of the activities of Rho from different pathogens. (A) Bar diagram showing the fractions of ATP hydrolyzed by each of the Rho proteins in the absence and presence of 1 μM and 5 μM Psu. In control experiments, the Psu ΔCTD10 mutant was used at 5 μM. As in Fig. 2 , poly(rC) was used as the substrate for all the Rho proteins except for those from E. coli and Salmonella . For E. coli , an RNA with a λ t R1 terminator was used, whereas rC 25 oligonucleotide RNA was used for Salmonella Rho protein. Errors were calculated from 3 independent measurements. (B) (Left) Autoradiograms showing the results of in vitro Rho-dependent transcription termination assays, using Rho proteins of E. coli , S. enterica , and M. tuberculosis , performed on a DNA template with the λ t R1 terminator (see the adjacent cartoon), both in the absence and in the presence of 5 μM Psu in the reaction mixture. The termination zone is indicated. (Right) Bar diagram showing the fractions of runoff (RO) products under different conditions. This is calculated using the following formula: (intensity of the runoff product)/(intensities of all the terminated products + intensity of the runoff product). Errors were calculated from three independent measurements. (C) Cartoon showing the stalled transcription elongation complex (TEC) at a lac repressor roadblock located downstream of the trpt ′ terminator. On this template, transcription is initiated from the T7A1 promoter, and RNA release is induced from the stalled EC by different Rho proteins as indicated in panel D. The template is immobilized via attachment to a magnetic bead to monitor the released RNA. (D) Autoradiogram showing RNA release in the S fractions (half of the supernatant) and the P fractions (the other half of the supernatant and the whole of the pellet). RNA products in different fractions are shown in the presence and absence of Psu. The times of incubation of the stalled ECs with different Rho proteins are indicated. RB, RNA product of the stalled EC. (E) Bar diagram showing the amounts of RNA released under different conditions in panel D. Fractions of released RNA were calculated as follows: 2S/[S + (S+P)]. Errors were measured from three independent experiments.

Psu is capable of inhibiting E. coli Rho-dependent termination in an in vitro purified system ( 11 ). Next, we assayed the Psu-mediated inhibition of the transcription termination functions of the different Rho proteins chosen for this study by use of the E. coli transcription system. We used a linear DNA template carrying the λ t R1 terminator sequence cloned downstream of a strong T7A1 promoter ( Fig. 3B ). On this template, a 22-mer EC (EC 23 ) was made first and was then chased with 20 μM nucleoside triphosphates (NTPs) through the terminator region in the presence of Rho alone or Rho with WT Psu ( Fig. 3B ). We used lower concentrations of NTPs to check the activity of Psu. Under this stringent condition, E. coli Rho is very efficient at termination, but among the other Rho proteins, only those from M. tuberculosis , S. enterica ( Fig. 3B ), and C. glutamicum (see Fig. S2B in the supplemental material) were able to terminate an elongating E. coli RNA polymerase (RNAP) (for the inability of the other Rho proteins, see Fig. S2A). In the presence of WT Psu, significant fractions of transcripts were found to read through the terminator regions ( Fig. 3B , left, lanes 3, 5, and 7, and right) when the M. tuberculosis and S. enterica Rho proteins were used, whereas the effect of Psu on C. glutamicum Rho was moderate (Fig. S2B). These results indicate that Psu is capable of inhibiting the termination function of the Rho proteins from these pathogens. Since the majority of the Rho proteins were not able to terminate the elongating E. coli RNAP, we hypothesized that these Rho proteins might be able to release RNA from a stalled elongation complex (EC), since kinetic coupling between Rho and the RNAP is not required if the latter is stalled on the DNA template. Thus, we designed a setup where the EC is stalled on a template bound to magnetic beads, at a particular position inside the trpt ′ terminator (an E. coli Rho-dependent terminator) region, by use of the lac repressor as a roadblock ( Fig. 3C ). In this setup, the terminated RNA would be released in the supernatant. After the EC was stalled, all the NTPs were removed by washing the beads, following which Rho and ATP were added either in the absence or in the presence of Psu, and the RNA release was observed in the supernatant ( Fig. 3D ). In the absence of WT Psu, Rho proteins from all the organisms except Pseudomonas (Fig. S2C) could release RNA very efficiently. It should be noted that most of the Rho proteins from pathogens took a longer time to release RNA from a divergent EC made of the E. coli RNAP ( Fig. 3D and E ). In the presence of WT Psu, RNA release from the stalled EC by all the Rho proteins used in this study was significantly reduced ( Fig. 3D and E ). RNA polymerase release assays (equivalent to RNA release assays) with the Psu ΔCTD10 mutant showed that in contrast to its WT counterpart, the mutant was unable to inhibit the dislodging of the polymerases from the stalled ECs by the various Rho proteins (see Fig. S3A in the supplemental material). These observations strongly indicate that WT Psu is capable of specifically inhibiting the Rho proteins from several pathogenic species in a manner comparable to that observed for E. coli Rho inhibition by this bacteriophage protein. It should also be noted that the Psu-induced inhibition of the RNA release efficiencies by different Rho proteins is qualitatively correlated with the extent of inhibition observed for their respective ATPase assays. Since the Rho-dependent termination function is a fundamental biological process of the prokaryotic world, we postulate that Psu would be able to inhibit these Rho proteins in the presence of their respective transcription systems. Since Pseudomonas Rho was not able to release RNA from this stalled EC, we refrain from concluding that Psu would be an efficient inhibitor of the Rho protein of this bacterium.

Previously, the formation of a specific Psu- E. coli Rho complex was demonstrated only in vivo by overexpressing both the proteins together in the same strain ( 11 , 13 , 14 ). We failed to obtain a stable in vitro complex with the purified components. This is most likely due to the misfolding propensity of the unusually knotted dimer conformation of Psu ( 12 ). Following the same procedure as before ( 11 ), we cooverexpressed His-tagged Psu and non-His-tagged WT Rho from two different pET vectors and pulled down the complex using Ni-nitrilotriacetic acid (Ni-NTA) beads. Typically, after induction, the cell lysate was directly loaded onto Ni-NTA columns. The eluted fractions of WT Psu contained significant amounts of E. coli , S. enterica , and V. cholerae Rho proteins ( Fig. 4A ). For X. campestris and X. oryzae Rho, both Rho and Psu were overexpressed in separate strains, and then the two lysates were mixed and loaded onto the Ni-NTA column. In all these cases, ∼30% of Rho proteins were found to be associated with Psu, a level comparable with what we observed for E. coli Rho. P. syringae Rho was unable to bind to Psu even under in vivo conditions, which, again, indicated that Psu might not be an efficient antagonist of this Rho protein (Fig. S3B). These interactions were specific, as evidenced by the fact that they were 4- to 7-fold weaker when the ΔCTD10 Psu mutant, defective at inhibiting the Rho protein ( 11 ), was used in the various experiments ( Fig. 4B ). The expression levels of M. tuberculosis and C. glutamicum Rho proteins were too low to allow the measurement of complex formation by this procedure. These results suggest in vivo complex formation by Psu with some of the Rho proteins, at least in the cytoplasm of E. coli , and we concluded that this interaction plays an important role in inhibiting the Rho function. FIG 4 FIG 4 Complex formation by Psu with different Rho proteins. In vivo pulldown assays show direct complex formation between different Rho proteins and WT Psu (A) or ΔCTD10 Psu (B). Flowthrough (FT) and wash (W) fractions contain the unbound Rho, whereas the amounts of protein in the eluate (E) fraction give the measure of the Psu-bound Rho. Both the protein bands are indicated. Fractions of Rho associated were calculated as follows: [E]/([FT] + [W] + [E]). Values are shown below the gel images. Errors were calculated from two or three independent measurements.

So far, we have established that the bacteriophage P4 coat protein Psu is capable of inhibiting the in vitro functions of Rho proteins from a diverse set of bacteria, most likely via direct interaction with the Rho proteins. In order to design a potent inhibitor of Rho from Psu, it is imperative that the latter's expression in different bacterial strains should cause lethality or toxicity. We chose two of the Mycobacterium (Gram-positive) strains and one of the Xanthomonas (Gram-negative) strains to study the in vivo effects of Psu, based on their availability and ease of handling. Since Salmonella Rho complements the deficiency of E. coli Rho in vivo , we used an E. coli strain expressing Salmonella Rho to monitor the in vivo effects of Psu. Since the Rho proteins of M. tuberculosis and M. bovis have the same sequence, and the M. bovis strains are easier to handle than the M. tuberculosis strains, we monitored the in vivo effect of Psu in an M. bovis strain. The Rho proteins of Salmonella enterica and E. coli are 99% homologous, and thus, the former can complement E. coli Rho in vivo . We deleted the chromosomal copy of rho from an E. coli MG1655 strain and supplied S. enterica Rho from a pHYD3011 plasmid (pRS1801). Subsequently, the strain was transformed with an isopropyl-β- d -thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG)-inducible plasmid that had WT psu cloned under the control of a P tac promoter ( Fig. 5A ). When this strain was streaked onto plates containing increasing concentrations of IPTG, causing Psu expression, severe growth inhibition was observed ( Fig. 5A ), similar to what was observed for E. coli Rho previously ( 11 , 14 ). This indicated that Psu inhibits S. enterica Rho efficiently in vivo . FIG 5 FIG 5 Psu-induced lethality of different bacteria. (A) MG1655 Δ rho :: kan strains expressing Salmonella enterica Rho from the pHYD3011 plasmid were transformed together with a second plasmid expressing WT Psu. Strains were streaked onto LB plates in the presence and absence of different concentrations of the inducer, IPTG, as indicated. (B) Xanthomonas campestris strain Xcc8004 (WT) producing the inducer DSF and Xcc8523, a DSF-deficient strain ( rpfF mutant), were transformed with pRS1574 (a Xanthomonas shuttle vector), pKLN55 (expressing WT Psu), or pRS1508 (empty pKLN55 vector). The Xcc8004 strain was also transformed with pRS1845 expressing ΔCTD20 Psu. Plates with the transformants are shown. The cartoon above the plates shows the location of psu under the control of P eng . The transformants obtained upon transformation of strain Xcc8004 with pRS1508 and pRS1845 were streaked separately to show the inability of the Psu mutant to cause mortality. (C) Mycobacterium smegmatis strain mc 2 155 was transformed with either a pSTKT empty vector (pRS1511) or a plasmid expressing either WT Psu (pRS1724) or the Psu ΔCTD20 mutant (pRS1837). Transformants were subsequently patched onto 7H10 plates. The location of the WT or mutant psu gene under the control of the ATc (anhydrous tetracycline)-inducible promoter P tet is shown in a cartoon. (D) The Mycobacterium bovis BCG strain was transformed with the plasmids described in the legend to panel C. Transformants obtained after 3 weeks are shown, and in two of the cases, colonies were further streaked to show the growth differences in the M. bovis strains upon expression of either WT or mutant Psu protein. Xanthomonas campestris is a Gram-negative plant pathogen (infecting cabbage, mustard, etc.) that produces a diffusible signal factor (DSF) responsible for cell-cell communication and different pathogenic aspects of the bacterium. We cloned the WT and ΔCTD20 (coding for a 20-amino-acid deletion in the C-terminal domain [ 11 ]) psu genes under the control of a DSF-inducible promoter ( P eng ) so that the naturally produced DSF would induce the expression of Psu in X. campestris ( Fig. 5B ). We transformed two strains, Xcc8004 (DSF producing) and Xcc8523 ( rpfF mutant; DSF deficient), with either pRS1574 (WT psu ), pRS1845 (ΔCTD20 psu ), or pRS1508 (an empty vector). WT Xcc8004 did not produce any transformants when transformed with pRS1574, whereas Xcc8523 produced healthy colonies under the same conditions. Healthy colonies were also formed by both the strains when they were transformed with the empty vector, pRS1508, or with the Psu mutant expressing pRS1845 ( Fig. 5B ). These observations indicated that rho is essential in X. campestris and that its specific inhibition by Psu leads to cell mortality. We used M. smegmatis strain mc 2 155 and M. bovis BCG to monitor the effect of in vivo expression of Psu. We electroporated M. smegmatis with pSTKT plasmids containing either WT psu (pRS1724) or ΔCTD20 psu (pRS1837; encoding a 20-amino-acid deletion at the C-terminal domain), or with an empty vector (pRS1511). In this vector, the psu genes are cloned under the control of an anhydrous tetracycline (ATc)-inducible P tet promoter. The WT psu transformants were very small and few in number, and upon restreaking, they exhibited very poor growth, whereas the transformants expressing ΔCTD20 psu or carrying only the vector grew well upon restreaking ( Fig. 5C ). We also performed similar experiments using the M. bovis BCG strain. There were very few transformants, with heterogeneous sizes, when the strain was transformed with pRS1724, whereas a high number of transformants was observed when M. bovis BCG was transformed with either pRS1837 or pRS1511 ( Fig. 5D ). Upon restreaking of these transformants, colonies expressing WT psu showed very poor growth compared to those expressing mutant psu . Interestingly, in all the cases described ( Fig. 5C and D ), WT Psu inhibited growth even in the absence of the inducer, ATc. Basal-level expression of Psu was sufficient to cause severe growth defects in both of these Mycobacterium strains. Therefore, we concluded that Psu functions as a strong inhibitor of Mycobacterium Rho in vivo .

DISCUSSION.content: The bacteriophage P4 capsid coat protein moonlights as a strong inhibitor of theE. colitranscription terminator Rho (11). Rho is a highly conserved protein among the prokaryotes (Fig. 1), and hence, we hypothesized that Psu may function as a “universal” inhibitor of this transcription terminator from different bacteria. Here we tested the inhibitory properties of the Psu protein against a diverse set of Rho proteins (Fig. 1) and showed unequivocally that Psu efficiently antagonizes the two most important functions of Rho, namely, ATPase activity and the transcription termination function (Fig. 3), indicating direct complex formation between the Psu and Rho proteins from different species. We demonstrated this complex formationin vivowith the Rho proteins from four representative bacteria (Fig. 4). Most importantly, we showed thatin vivoexpression of the Psu protein induces mortality in four pathogenic bacteria by antagonizing their transcription termination function (Fig. 5). We concluded from these results that thisE. colibacteriophage protein is capable of functioning as a universal antagonist of transcription termination in a wide range of bacteria (both Gram positive and Gram negative). We hypothesize that this phage protein could be useful for designing peptide inhibitors of Rho.

It is noteworthy that Psu functions across genus boundaries, considering that P4 is a phage with such a narrow host range. Most of the bacteriophage-derived protein factors target conserved host machineries, such as RNA polymerases. The recently solved structures of theE. colitranscription modulators from the lambdoid phages, N (19) and Nun (20), in complex with the transcription elongation complex (TEC) revealed that these factors target conserved regions around the active center of the RNAP. Hence, it could be predicted that these transcription regulators would also function with RNA polymerases from other bacteria. Similarly, even though Psu is a unique protein ofE. coliphage P4, it targets the universal ATPase domain present in this conserved transcription termination factor, Rho, in different bacteria. Therefore, the host range of the phages and the nature of the cellular targets may not necessarily be correlated.

The Rho protein could be a potent drug target for the following reasons. (i) It is structurally and functionally highly conserved in most bacteria (1), making it possible to design drugs, based on the structure ofE. coliRho, for the Rho proteins from different pathogens (15). (ii) Rho-dependent termination is involved in many important physiological processes (9), and affecting any of them could cause lethality for the bacteria. (iii) It has been shown recently that Rho-dependent termination is involved in the pathogenicity ofMycobacterium tuberculosis(21). Bicyclomycin is the only known antibiotic that binds to Rho (22–24), but its application is limited due to its inability to cross the cell membrane of the Gram-positive pathogens and its high cytotoxicity (25,26). With this background, the present study establishing the potential of Psu to inhibit Rho proteins from a wide variety of pathogens could enable us to design a new Rho inhibitor(s). A drug compromising the Rho function might also be used as a component of a synergistic treatment regime together with other antibiotics, which would have great implications for the treatment of multidrug-resistant (MDR) and extensively drug resistant (XDR) bacterial strains.

We have shown previously that the helical C-terminal 20-amino-acid region of Psu makes direct contacts with Rho (12,13). Biochemical probing and the crystal structure of Psu have revealed (13) that helices 6 and 7 forming the C-terminal Rho binding region are surface exposed and form the tip of the arms of the V-shaped knotted dimer of Psu. It appears from the structure that the rest of the Psu dimer functions as a scaffold to hold these helices nearer to the Psu-binding loop region (Fig. 1) of Rho so that the local concentrations of the helices increase due to the reduction of the entropy of the C-terminal domain of the former. We speculate that the sequences of these helices could function as a template for the design of peptide inhibitors of the Rho protein. Ideally, these peptide sequences must have higher affinities for the Rho protein so that they can bind in the absence of the rest of the Psu scaffold. It would be interesting to know whether these two helices or their sequence variations in isolation could interact with Rho and elicit the antagonizing function.

The bacteriophages are large reservoirs of unique proteins that are capable of modulating many bacterial machineries for their own advantage. Like Psu, most of these protein modulators have no homology with known proteins from any of the three kingdoms. They usually modulate host machineries through direct interactions with the key host proteins and regulatory DNA and RNA sequences, leading to complete shutdown of the host metabolism and eventual killing. In addition to the effectiveness of these phage modulators, they are small, which makes them amenable to functioning as a platform for the design of new peptide inhibitors. A crude estimate takes the number of bacteriophage particles to be 1031(27). Therefore, theoretically, this reservoir is capable of producing “infinite” numbers of these modulators specific for each of the pathogenic bacteria, and the number surpasses the capacity of the chemical libraries that are presently in use. We envision that the bacteriophage-modulator libraries, products of natural origin, could become a cheaper alternative to the chemical libraries.

MATERIALS AND METHODS.content: NTPs were purchased from GE Healthcare. [γ- 32 P]ATP (3,000 Ci/mmol) and [α- 32 P]CTP (3,000 Ci/mmol) were obtained from Jonaki, BRIT (Hyderabad, India). Antibiotics, IPTG, lysozyme, dithiothreitol (DTT), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were from U.S. Biochemical Corp. Restriction endonucleases, polynucleotide kinase, and T4 DNA ligase were from New England BioLabs. WT E. coli RNA polymerase holoenzyme was purchased from Epicentre Biotechnologies and New England BioLabs. Streptavidin-coated magnetic beads were from Promega. Taq DNA polymerase was obtained from Roche Applied Science. Ni-NTA–agarose beads were from Qiagen. Details of the bacterial strains and plasmids are described in Table 1 . Genomic DNA was isolated from the pathogens Salmonella enterica , Corynebacterium glutamicum , Xanthomonas oryzae , Xanthomonas campestris , and Pseudomonas syringae using the genomic DNA isolation kit (Invitrogen). The rho genes were PCR amplified from the genomic DNA using Deep Vent DNA polymerase (NEB) and were cloned in NdeI-XhoI sites of the pET28b vector. All the clones were sequenced. E. coli and M. tuberculosis Rho were available from the lab stock ( 11 , 17 ). Vibrio cholerae Rho cloned into pET28a was a gift from U. Sen (SINP, Kolkata, India). The Rho proteins were overexpressed in BL21(DE3) cells and were purified using Ni-NTA beads (Qiagen) as per the manufacturer's protocol. The proteins eluted from the Ni-NTA columns were further purified by passing through a HiTrap Heparin HP Sepharose column (GE Healthcare).

The rates of RNA-dependent ATP hydrolysis of Rho proteins of different bacterial species were measured using poly(rC) as the template. ATP hydrolysis was assayed by monitoring the release of P i from ATP that was observed on polyethylenimine thin-layer chromatography (TLC) plates using 0.75 M KH 2 PO 4 (pH 3.5) as a mobile-phase buffer. The hydrolysis reactions were performed in T buffer (25 mM Tris-HCl [pH 8.0], 50 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl 2 , 1 mM DTT, and 0.1 mg/ml BSA) at 37°C. The rate of ATP hydrolysis of 1 mM ATP mixed with [γ- 32 P]ATP (3,500 Ci/mmol; BRIT, India) was measured using 50 nM each Rho from different bacterial species. The reactions were initiated by the addition of 20 μM RNA. Aliquots were removed, and the reactions were stopped with 1.5 M formic acid at various time points. Release of P i was analyzed by exposing the TLC sheets to a PhosphorImager screen and subsequently by scanning using an FLA 9000 system (Typhoon). Psu-mediated inhibition of the Rho ATPase activities was measured using λ t R1 RNA as the template for E. coli Rho. This RNA has one of the strongest rut sites for E. coli Rho protein. Poly(rC) was used for all other Rho proteins except Salmonella Rho. Oligonucleotide rC 25 was used for S. enterica Rho. Reactions were initiated with the Rho proteins in the presence or absence of the WT and ΔCTD10 Psu proteins and were stopped at a final time point where maximum hydrolysis occurred. Other reaction conditions were same as those described above.

Linear DNA templates for in vitro transcription and RNA release assays were made by PCR amplification from plasmids pRS604 (P T7A1 -λ t R1 ) and pRS106 (P T7A1 -trpt ′) using the RS58/RS147 and RS83/RS177 oligonucleotide pairs, respectively. To form a roadblock (RB) on the template, a 22-bp lac operator sequence was inserted after the trpt ′ terminator sequence using a downstream primer (RS177) carrying the lac operator sequence ( 28 ). A transcription elongation roadblock is formed at this sequence in the presence of the lac repressor. To immobilize the DNA templates onto the streptavidin-coated magnetic beads (Promega), a biotin group at the 5′ end of the templates was incorporated by using a biotinylated primer (RS83).

In vitro transcription assays were performed on a P T7A1 -λ t R1 template, where transcription is initiated from the strong T7A1 promoter and elongation occurs through the Rho-dependent terminator λ t R1 . Transcription reactions were performed in the presence of rifampin in the transcription buffer (T-buffer; 25 mM Tris-HCl [pH 8.0], 5 mM MgCl 2 , 50 mM KCl, 1 mM DTT, and 0.1 mg/ml BSA). At first, a 23-mer elongation complex, EC 23 , was formed by mixing 5 nM DNA template, 25 nM RNA polymerase, 175 μM adenylyl (3′-5′)-uridine, 5 μM (each) ATP and GTP, and 2.5 μM CTP. [α- 32 P]CTP (3,000 Ci/mmol; BRIT, Hyderabad, India) was used for labeling the transcripts. This EC was then chased with 20 μM NTPs for 15 min. Samples were run on an 8% sequencing gel and were analyzed by an FLA 9000 PhosphorImager (Typhoon).

For the RNA release assays from the stalled ECs (RB), the P T7A1 - trpt ′- lacO template immobilized on streptavidin-coated magnetic beads was used. A 100 nM concentration of the lac repressor was added to the DNA templates to form an RB. On this template, at first the EC 23 was formed by initiating the reactions with 175 μM adenylyl (3′-5′)-uridine, 5 mM GTP, 5 mM ATP, 2.5 mM CTP, and [α- 32 P]CTP (3,000 Ci/mmol). This complex was then chased with 250 μM (each) NTPs. Following the chase, excess NTPs were removed by washing the beads thoroughly. Rho (50 nM) and Psu (5 μM) (when required) were added to the RB, and 10 μl of samples was removed at the indicated time points for each Rho (which differed for different Rho proteins) and was separated into S (half of the supernatant) and P (the other half of the supernatant plus the pellet) on the magnetic stand. The rest of the procedures were the same as those described above. The fractions of released RNA {2S/[S + (S+P)]} were measured for each of the Rho proteins.

RNA polymerase release from the stalled ECs (RB) on the P T7A1 - trpt ′- lacO template immobilized on streptavidin-coated magnetic beads was carried out by following procedures similar to those described above. The radioactive CTP was not included in these experiments. A 50 nM concentration of each type of Rho, 5 μM WT Psu, or 5 μM ΔCTD10 Psu was added to the RB, and 10 μl of samples was removed at the indicated time points. The supernatant (S) and pellet (P) fractions were separated by keeping the samples against a magnetic stand; the S fraction was removed directly, whereas the P fractions were resuspended in an equal volume of the T buffer. These two fractions were then loaded onto an SDS-PAGE gel and were analyzed by Western blotting. RNA polymerase release was detected using an anti-RpoC monoclonal antibody (NeoClone).

Strain BL21(DE3) was cotransformed with plasmids pET28 (Kan r ), expressing WT or ΔCTD Psu protein, and pET21 (Amp r ), expressing Rho proteins from E. coli , S. enterica , and V. cholerae . Psu proteins were His tagged at the N terminus. The transformants were inoculated in 5 ml of LB and were grown at 37°C for ∼3 h. The 5-ml culture was then added to 100 ml of LB and was grown to an optical density at 600 nm (OD 600 ) of ∼0.3, following which 0.1 mM IPTG was added to induce protein expression, and induction was continued for 3 h. The cells were then lysed in the lysis buffer (100 mM NaH 2 PO 4 , 100 mM NaCl, 10 mM imidazole, 1 mg/ml of lysozyme, and 10 mg/ml of phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride [PMSF]). The lysate was passed through Ni-NTA (Qiagen) affinity columns and was washed with wash buffer (100 mM NaH 2 PO 4 , 100 mM NaCl, and 20 mM imidazole), and the proteins were eluted with the elution buffer (100 mM NaH 2 PO 4 , 100 mM NaCl, and 500 mM imidazole). The volumes of lysate, wash buffer, and elution buffers were kept the same for loading of the same amount of proteins in each lane. Rho proteins of X. oryzae , X. campestris , M. tuberculosis , and C. glutamicum could not be cooverexpressed with Psu. Thus, the Rho proteins of Xanthomonas species and both types of Psu proteins were overexpressed separately, and then the lysates of the two were mixed, and pulldown was carried out in the same way as described above. The mixing was done for at least 2 h. The expression levels of the Rho proteins of M. tuberculosis and C. glutamicum were very low, so the mixing experiments were not attempted.

In vivo growth inhibition assays. (i) S. enterica Rho inhibition in E. coli . S. enterica rho was cloned into pHYD3011 under the control of the P BAD promoter. pHYD3011carrying S. enterica rho was transformed into the E. coli MG1655 WT strain. The chromosomal rho gene was subsequently deleted from the MG1655 strain. S. enterica rho complemented E. coli rho after the deletion of the chromosomal rho . These strains were then transformed with pNL150 expressing psu cloned under the control of an IPTG-inducible promoter, P tac , and the transformants were streaked onto plates containing different IPTG concentrations.

(ii) M. smegmatis mc 2 155 and M. bovis . WT psu and Δ20 CTD psu (coding for a 20-amino-acid deletion at the C-terminal domain) were PCR amplified from the pNL150- psu plasmid by Deep Vent DNA polymerase (NEB) and were cloned into the EcoRI/HindIII sites of plasmid pSTKT, a mycobacterial/ E. coli shuttle vector carrying P tetO (ATc [anhydrous tetracycline] inducible). Next, we electroporated WT andΔ20 CTD psu and the empty vector into the M. smegmatis mc 2 155 strain according to the published protocol ( 29 ). Transformants were grown at 37°C for 4 days in Difco Middlebrook 7H10 agar plates supplemented with 10% oleic acid-albumin-dextrose-catalase (OADC) and 0.2% glycerol in the presence of 10 μg/ml kanamycin and were further purified in a higher concentration (25 μg/ml) of a kanamycin-containing medium. Subsequently, colonies were patched onto plates to study the effect of psu expression on the viability of M. smegmatis cells. In the same way, the M. bovis strain was transformed with plasmids carrying different psu derivatives by following the procedures described above. The transformants were grown for 3 weeks under the same conditions as those used for M. smegmatis . The transformants expressing WT or mutant Psu proteins were restreaked subsequently.

(iii) X. campestris and X. oryzae . The WT and ΔCTD20 psu genes were cloned into the SalI/HindIII sites of pKLN55, a Xanthomonas shuttle vector carrying a P eng promoter (a DSF [diffusible signal factor]-inducible endoglucanase gene promoter). X. campestris strains Xcc8004 (WT; DSF producing) and Xcc8523 ( rpfF mutant; not producing DSF) were electroporated with the pKLN55 plasmids with or without the WT or mutant psu gene. The transformants were grown in peptone-sucrose-agar medium at 28°C.

All sequences of Rho proteins from the selected bacterial species were submitted to the Clustal Omega online server ( 30 ) to obtain the multiple-sequence alignment. The phylogenetic tree was built from the multiple-sequence alignment of 16S RNA by using SeaView software ( 31 ) and by the BioNJ distance method ( 32 ). Homology modeling of all the Rho proteins was performed by MODELLER software, version 9v17 ( 33 ). The E. coli Rho crystal structure (PDB code 3ICE ) was used as the template for the modeling. The best model was selected on the basis of DOPE (discrete optimized protein energy), a statistical method optimized for model assessment. All Rho monomers from different species were superimposed in the UCSF Chimera software, version 1.11 ( 34 ), and the Psu-binding regions were highlighted in each of the Rho monomers.
